Chapter 17: Payout Policy
- 17.1 Distributions to Shareholders
- 17.2Comparison of Dividends and Share Repurchases
- 17.3The Tax Disadvantage of Dividends
- 17.4Dividend Capture and Tax Clienteles
- 17.5Payout Versus Retention of Cash
- 17.6 Signaling with Payout Policy
- 17.7 Stock Dividends, Splits and Spin-offs

NTNU

www.ntnu.no
Learning Objectives
▪ Discuss the effect of dividend payment or share repurchase in perfect capital markets.
▪ Assuming perfect capital markets, describe what Modigliani and Miller (1961) found about payout policy.
▪ Describe th effect of tax on payout policy
▪ Provide reasons why firms might accumulate cash balances rather than pay dividends.
▪ Describe the effect of agency costs on payout policy.
▪ Assess the impact of information asymmetry on payout policy.

NTNU
Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
www.ntnu.no
Distribution to Shareholders
Payout Policy
▪ The way a firm chooses between the alternative ways to distribute free cash flow to equity holders

Free Cash Flow
  - Retain
    - Invest in New Projects
    - Increase Cash Reserves
  - Pay Out
    - Repurchase Shares
    - Pay Dividends

# Chapter 17: Payout Policy

- 17.1 Distributions to Shareholders

- 17.2Comparison of Dividends and Share Repurchases

- 17.3The Tax Disadvantage of Dividends

- 17.4Dividend Capture and Tax Clienteles

- 17.5Payout Versus Retention of Cash

## Learning Objectives

▪ Discuss the effect of dividend payment or share repurchase in perfect capital markets.

▪ Assuming perfect capital markets, describe what Modigliani and Miller (1961) found about payout policy.

▪ Describe th effect of tax on payout policy

▪ Provide reasons why firms might accumulate cash balances rather than pay dividends.

▪ Describe the effect of agency costs on payout policy.

▪ Assess the impact of information asymmetry on payout policy.



NTNU
Norwegian University of
Science and Technology

www.ntnu.no

54 54 54

## Distribution to Shareholders

## Payout Policy

▪ The way a firm chooses between the alternative ways to distribute free cash flow to equity holders

Free Cash Flow
  - Retain
    - Invest in New Projects
    - Increase Cash Reserves
  - Pay Out
    - Repurchase Shares
    - Pay Dividends

www.ntnu.no

▪ The date on which the board of directors authorizes the payment of a dividend Ex-dividend Date
▪ A date, two days prior to a dividend’s record date, on or after which anyone buying the stock will not be eligible for the dividend

▪ When a firm pays a dividend, only shareholders on record on this date receive the dividend.

55# Chapter 17: Payout Policy (cont.)

## Dividends

This table outlines the key dates for a special dividend of $3.00 per share. The dividend was declared on July 20, 2004, with an ex-dividend date of November 15, 2004. Shareholders must be recorded by November 17, 2004, to be eligible to receive their payment on December 2, 2004.

## Declaration Date

▪ The date on which the board of directors authorizes the payment of a dividend Ex-dividend Date

	▪ A date, two days prior to a dividend’s record date, on or after which anyone buying the stock will not be eligible for the dividend

## Record Date

▪ When a firm pays a dividend, only shareholders on record on this date receive the dividend.

## Payable Date (Distribution Date)

▪ A date, generally within a month after the record date, on which dividend is paid

www.ntnu.no

## Share Repurchases

▪ An alternative way to pay cash to investors is through a share repurchase or buyback.

	▪ The firm uses cash to buy shares o

blocks.content: Share Repurchases
▪ An alternative way to pay cash to investors is through a share repurchase or buyback.
▪ The firm uses cash to buy shares of its own outstanding stock.

▪ There are different transaction types for share
repurchases (see Chapter 17.1)

NTNU
Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
www.ntnu.no

Comparison of Dividends
and Share Repurchases
Example of Genron Corporation in Section 17.2:
▪ The firm’s board is meeting to decide how to pay out $20 million in excess cash to shareholders.
▪ Genron has no debt, its equity cost of capital equals its unlevered cost of capital of 12%.
▪ Genron expects to generate future free cash flows of $48 million per year in subsequent years.
▪ Genron has 10 million outstanding shares.

NTNU
Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
www.ntnu.no

Comparison of Dividends
and Share Repurchases
Compare different potential policies:
▪ Pay dividend with excess cash
▪ Share repurchase (no dividend)
▪ High Dividend (equity issue)
Questions:
▪ Which policy would shareholders prefer?
▪ Will the amount of the dividend affect Genron’s share price?

NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
www.ntnu.no

Alternative Policy 1: Pay
Dividend with Excess Cash
▪ With 10 million shares outstanding, Genron will be able to pay a $2 dividend immediately.
▪ The firm expects to generate future free cash flows of $48 million per year, thus it anticipates paying a dividend of $4.80 per share each year thereafter.
▪ The price of Genron will be

4.80
P cum = Current Dividend + PV (Future Dividends) = 2 + 0.12 = 2 +40 = $42

NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
www.ntnu.no

and Share Repurchases

▪ Share repurchase (no dividend)

▪ High Dividend (equity issue)

▪ Which policy would shareholders prefer?

▪ Will the amount of the dividend affect Genron’s share price?

▪ The price of Genron will be

4.80
P cum = Current Dividend + PV (Future Dividends) = 2 + 0.12 = 2 +40 = $42

Alternative Policy 1: Pay
Dividend with Excess Cash
▪ In a perfect capital market, when a dividend is paid, the share price drops by the amount of the dividend when the stock begins to trade ex-dividend.

NTNU
Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
www.ntnu.no

Alternative Policy 2: Share
Repurchase (No Dividend)
▪ Suppose that instead of paying a dividend this year, Genron uses the $20 million to repurchase its shares on the open market.
▪ With an initial share price of $42, Genron will repurchase 476,000 shares.
$20 million $42 per share = 0.476 million shares
▪ This will leave only 9.524 million shares outstanding.
▪ 10 million − 0.476 million = 9.524 million

NTNU

Science and Technology

This table illustrates the impact of a cash dividend on the market value and share price of a security. On December 11th, the security had a total market value of $420 million, which included $20 million in cash. After the ex-dividend date of December 12th, the cash was distributed, reducing the total market value to $400 million and consequently lowering the share price from $42 to $40 per share.



NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology

www.ntnu.no

63

# Alternative Policy 1: Pay
Dividend with Excess Cash

▪ In a perfect capital market, when a dividend is paid, the share price drops by the amount of the dividend when the stock begins to trade ex-dividend.

## Alternative Policy 2: Share
Repurchase (No Dividend)

-----

▪ Suppose that instead of paying a dividend this year, Genron uses the $20 million to repurchase its shares on the open market.

	▪ With an initial share price of $42, Genron will repurchase 476,000 shares.

		$20 million $42 per share = 0.476 million shares

	▪ This will leave only 9.524 million shares outstanding.

	▪ 10 million − 0.476 million = 9.524 million



NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology

www.ntnu.no

65# Alternative Policy 2: Share
Repurchase (No Dividend)

▪ The net effect is that the share price remains unchanged.

▪ It should not be surprising that the repurchase had not effect on the stock price.

Alternative Policy 1
compared to 2:
■ Investor Preferences

▪ In perfect capital markets, investors are indifferent between the firm distributing funds via dividends or share repurchases. By reinvesting dividends or selling shares, they can replicate either payout method on their own.

NTNU
Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
www.ntnu.no

65# Alternative Policy 2: Share
Repurchase (No Dividend)

▪ The net effect is that the share price remains unchanged.

This table illustrates the impact of a share repurchase on a company's financial standing. Following the repurchase, the company's cash decreased from $20 million to $0 million, and the number of outstanding shares reduced from 10 million to 9.524 million. Despite these changes, the share price remained constant at $42, resulting in a decrease in the total market value of assets from $420 million to $400 million.

High Dividend (Equity Issue)
▪ Suppose Genron wants to pay dividend larger than $2 per share right now, but it only has $20 million in cash today. It wants to pay out $48 million
▪ Thus, Genron needs an additional $28 million to pay the larger dividend now.
▪ To do this, the firm decides to raise the cash by selling new shares.

## Genron’s Dividends per Share Each Year Under the Three Alternative Policies

This table presents the initial share price and dividend payments for three different investment policies over three years. All policies start with an initial share price of $42.00. Policy 1 pays dividends of $2.00 in Year 0, followed by $4.80 in both Year 1 and Year 2. Policy 2 has no dividend in Year 0, but pays $5.04 in both Year 1 and Year 2. Policy 3 offers a consistent dividend of $4.50 per share across Year 0, Year 1, and Year 2.

be higher.

73# 17.3 The Tax Disadvantage of Dividends and 17.4 Tax Clienteles

▪ Taxes on Dividends and Capital Gains

	▪ Shareholders must pay taxes on the dividends they receive and they must also pay capital ga

blocks.content: 17.3 The Tax Disadvantage of Dividends and 17.4 Tax Clienteles
▪ Taxes on Dividends and Capital Gains
▪ Shareholders must pay taxes on the dividends they receive and they must also pay capital gains taxes when they sell their shares.
▪ In some countries dividends are taxed at a higher rate than capital gains. In that case shareholders might prefer share repurchases to dividends.
▪ In Norway, the tax system is symmetric, and hence there is no such incentive

NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
www.ntnu.no

17.5 Payout Versus Retention
of Cash
▪ MM Payout Irrelevance
▪ In perfect capital markets, if a firm invests excess cash flows in financial securities, the firm’s choice of payout versus retention is irrelevant and does not affect the initial share price.
▪ With market imperfections, there is a tradeoff: Retaining cash can reduce the costs of raising capital in the future, but it can also increase taxes and agency costs.

NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
www.ntnu.no
Example
▪ Payne Enterprises has $20,000,000 in excess cash.
▪ Payne is considering investing the cash in one-year Treasury bill paying 5% interest and then using the cash to pay a dividend next year.

▪ If Payne pays an immediate dividend, the shareholders receive $20,000,000
today.
▪ If Payne retains the cash, at the end of one year the company will be able to pay a dividend of $20,000,000 × (1.05) = $21,000,000
▪ If shareholders invest the $20,000,000 in Treasury bills themselves, they would $20,000,000× (1.05) = $21,000,000
▪ The present value in either scenario is $20,000,000
▪ Thus shareholders are indifferent about whether the firm pays the dividend immediately or retains the cash.

NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
www.ntnu.no

chunks.content:
the investment policy of a firm, the firm’s choice of dividend policy is irrelevant and does not affect the initial share price.

	▪ In Norway, the tax system is symmetric, and hence there is no such incentive

76# 17.3 The Tax Disadvantage of Dividends and 17.4 Tax Clienteles (cont.)

## Market imperfections

- Taxes

	▪ Corporate taxes make it costly for a firm to retain excess cash.

	▪ Cash is equivalent t

blocks.content: Market imperfections
- Taxes
▪ Corporate taxes make it costly for a firm to retain excess cash.
▪ Cash is equivalent to negative leverage, so the tax advantage of leverage implies a tax disadvantage to holding cash
▪ Issuance and Distress Costs
▪ Generally, firms retain cash balances to cover potential future cash shortfalls, despite the tax disadvantage to retaining cash.
▪ A firm might accumulate a large cash balance if there is a reasonable chance that future earnings will be insufficient to fund future positive- NPV investment opportunities.
▪ Firms should choose to retain to help with future growth opportunities and to avoid financial distress costs.
- Agency Costs
▪ When firms have excessive cash, managers may use the funds inefficiently by paying excessive executive perks, over-paying for acquisitions, etc.
▪ Paying out excess cash through dividends or share repurchases, rather than retaining cash, can boost the stock price by reducing managers’ ability and temptation to waste resources.

NTNU
☐ Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
www.ntnu.no

17.6 Signaling with Payout
Policy
▪ The idea that dividend changes reflect managers’ views about a firm’s future earning prospects.
▪ Dividend Smoothing
▪ The practice of maintaining relatively constant dividends

▪ Firm change dividends infrequently and dividends are much
less volatile than earnings
▪ Research has found that management believes that investors desire to maintain a long-term target level of dividends as a fraction of earnings.
▪ If firms smooth dividends, the firm’s dividend choice will contain information regarding management’s expectations of future earnings.

NTNU
Norwegian University of
Science and Technology
www.ntnu.no

▪ If investors believe that managers have better information regarding the firm’s prospects and act on behalf of current shareholders, then investors will react favorably to share repurchase announcements.

81# Practitioner's view

The company considered five key questions in developing its distribution strategy:

1. Can the company sustain payment of a cash dividend in perpetuity and increase the dividend

blocks.content: Practitioner's view
The company considered five key questions in developing its distribution strategy:
1. Can the company sustain payment of a cash dividend in perpetuity and increase the dividend over time?

2. Is a cash dividend a better return to stockholders than a stock buyback program? These are capital structure decisions: Do we want to reduce our shares outstanding? Is our stock attractively priced for a buyback, or do we want to distribute the cash as a dividend? Microsoft had plenty of capacity to issue a dividendandcontinue a buyback program.
3. What is the tax effect of a cash dividend versus a buyback to the corporation and to shareholders?
4. What is the psychological impact on investors, and how does it fit the story of the stock for investors? This is a more qualitative factor.

5. What are the public relations implications of a dividend program? Investors don’t look to Microsoft to hold cash but to be a leader in software development and provide equity growth. So they viewed the dividend program favorably.
From the interview with John Connors former CFO at Microsoft (you can find it in the book).

NTNU
Norwegian University of
Science and Technology

81# Practitioner's view

The company considered five key questions in developing its distribution strategy:

1. Can the company sustain payment of a cash dividend in perpetuity and increase the dividend over time?

2. Is a cash dividend a better return to stockholders than a stock buyback program? These are capital structure decisions: Do we want to reduce our shares outstanding? Is our stock attractively priced for a buyback, or do we want to distribute the cash as a dividend? Microsoft had plenty of capacity to issue a dividendandcontinue a buyback program.

3. What is the tax effect of a cash dividend versus a buyback to the corporation and to shareholders?

4. What is the psychological impact on investors, and how does it fit the story of the stock for investors? This is a more qualitative factor.

5. What are the public relations implications of a dividend program? Investors don’t look to Microsoft to hold cash but to be a leader in software development and provide equity growth. So they viewed the dividend program favorably.

From the interview with John Connors former CFO at Microsoft (you can find it in the book).

Science and Technology

81# Practitioner's view

The company considered five key questions in developing its distribution strategy:

1. Can the company sustain payment of a cash dividend in perpetuity and increase the dividend over time?

2. Is a cash dividend a better return to stockholders than a stock buyback program? These are capital structure decisions: Do we want to reduce our shares outstanding? Is our stock attractively priced for a buyback, or do we want to distribute the cash as a dividend? Microsoft had plenty of capacity to issue a dividendandcontinue a buyback program.

3. What is the tax effect of a cash dividend versus a buyback to the corporation and to shareholders?

4. What is the psychological impact on investors, and how does it fit the story of the stock for investors? This is a more qualitative factor.

5. What are the public relations implications of a dividend program? Investors don’t look to Microsoft to hold cash but to be a leader in software development and provide equity growth. So they viewed the dividend program favorably.

82# Practitioner's view (cont.)

## Key take-aways Chapter 17

▪ In perfect capital markets, holding fixed the investment policy of a firm, the firm’s choice of dividend policy is irrelevant and does no

blocks.content: Key take-aways Chapter 17
▪ In perfect capital markets, holding fixed the investment policy of a firm, the firm’s choice of dividend policy is irrelevant and does not affect the initial share price.
▪ In perfect capital markets, if a firm invests excess cash flows in financial securities, the firm’s choice of payout versus retention is irrelevant and does not affect the initial share price.
▪ With market imperfections, there is a tradeoff: Retaining cash can reduce the costs of raising capital in the future, but it can also increase taxes and agency costs.
▪ Dividends and share repurchases help minimize the agency problem of wasteful spending when a firm has excess cash.
- Dividend signaling hypothesis.
▪ Managers usually increase dividends only when they are confident the firm will be able to afford higher dividends for the foreseeable future.
▪ When managers cut the dividend, it may signal that they have lost hope that earnings will improve.
▪ Share repurchases may be used to signal positive information, as repurchases are more attractive if management believes the stock is undervalued at its current price.

82# Practitioner's view (cont.)

## Key take-aways Chapter 17

▪ In perfect capital markets, holding fixed the investment policy of a firm, the firm’s choice of dividend policy is irrelevant and does not affect the initial share price.

▪ In perfect capital markets, if a firm invests excess cash flows in financial securities, the firm’s choice of payout versus retention is irrelevant and does not affect the initial share price.

▪ With market imperfections, there is a tradeoff: Retaining cash can reduce the costs of raising capital in the future, but it can also increase taxes and agency costs.

▪ Dividends and share repurchases help minimize the agency problem of wasteful spending when a firm has excess cash.

- Dividend signaling hypothesis.

	▪ Managers usually increase dividends only when they are confident the firm will be able to afford higher dividends for the foreseeable future.

	▪ When managers cut the dividend, it may signal that they have lost hope that earnings will improve.

	▪ Share repurchases may be used to signal positive information, as repurchases are more attractive if management believes the stock is undervalued at its current price.

▪ In perfect capital markets, if a firm invests excess cash flows in financial securities, the firm’s choice of payout versus retention is irrelevant and does not affect the initial share price.

▪ With market imperfections, there is a tradeoff: Retaining cash can reduce the costs of raising capital in the future, but it can also increase taxes and agency costs.

▪ Dividends and share repurchases help minimize the agency problem of wasteful spending when a firm has excess cash.

- Dividend signaling hypothesis.

	▪ Managers usually increase dividends only when they are confident the firm will be able to afford higher dividends for the foreseeable future.

	▪ When managers cut the dividend, it may signal that they have lost hope that earnings will improve.

	▪ Share repurchases may be used to signal positive information, as repurchases are more attractive if management believes the stock is undervalued at its current price.
